APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Plaintiff DR. BRIAN HANLEY (Tel: 415-518-8153) in pro per;
No other appearances made in this case.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT CENTIVAX, INC.'s Motion To Compel Compliance With February 21, 2023 Report Of Judge Pro Tem; Stipulation To Provide Further Response And Sanctions Against Brian Hanley.

The Court notes that plaintiff Dr. Hanley did not timely contest the Court's tentative ruling, and that there is no appearance by/for the defendant at today's hearing. As such, pursuant to the Local Rules, the Court indicates it is precluded from hearing argument, it must deem the parties to have stipulated to the pro tem judge, and it must adopt its tentative ruling.

The Court adopts its tentative ruling, as follows:

Defendant Centivax, Inc.'s motion to compel compliance with the February 21, 2023 Report of Judge Pro Tem ("February Report") is granted pursuant to the stipulation of plaintiff Brian Hanley's prior counsel, Christopher D. Sullivan and Sullivan Blackburn Pratt LLP ("SBP"). Dr. Hanley, now representing himself, is ordered to comply with the February Report within thirty (30) days of this signed order. The opposition from Dr. Hanley was filed late, but I exercise discretion to consider it. Centivax correctly argues that clients may be bound by their attorney, see Linsk v. Linsk, 70 Cal. 2d 272, 276 (1969), and Dr. Hanley's opposition does not provide any basis to deviate from this rule, such as that there was no agreement between Mr. Sullivan/SBP and Centivax counsel, or that Mr. Sullivan/SBP stipulated to comply with the February Report over Dr. Hanley's objection. Dr. Hanley's opposition questions the findings in the February Report, but the underlying substance of the February Report is beyond the scope of this motion given the stipulation between Mr. Sullivan/SBP and Centivax.
Both parties' requests for sanctions are denied. The record shows that Dr. Hanley began representing himself on July 7, 2023, and the one meet-and-confer letter (Exhibit D to Mr. Piser's Declaration), dated July 11, 2023, shows Dr. Hanley stating it is difficult for him to comply with the request because he is not yet in possession of the materials from his former attorney. Under these circumstances, I do not find that sanctions are warranted. Dr. Hanley seeks monetary sanctions and a protective order against the conduct of Centivax counsel, Mr. Piser, but I do not find the requested relief warranted based on the record before me. The Centivax Request for Judicial Notice is granted as to item 1, but denied as to item 2, as item 2 does not fall within Evid. Code Sec. 451, nor has Centivax furnished sufficient information to enable me to take notice. Evid. Code Secs. 452, 453.

The prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order to the pro tem judge at khuibonhoa@duanemorris.com.

Judge Pro Tem: Katherine Huibonhoa; Clerk: S. Kane; Not Reported. (302/JPT)

Hanley's request to continue the April 2, 2024, trial date is DENIED WITHOUT PREJUDICE. The trial calendar is maintained by the Presiding Judge. (See S.F.L.R. 6.0(A).) Where, as here, the Presiding Judge has set a trial date, the Presiding Judge determines motions for continuance of the trial date. (See S.F.L.R. 6.0(B).) "No motion for continuance of a trial date may be made or heard in any other department." (Id.) Thus, any party desiring a continuance of the April 2024 trial date must present their request to the Presiding Judge. The court advises counsel that it expects them to be with Mr. Haney, while he seeks new counsel.

MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Craig Byrnes, Esq. (310.706.4177) for Plaintiff.
No other appearances.

The court noted there was not timely compliance with tentative ruling rule. Mr. Byrnes confirmed the contest email was sent after 4PM. The Court passes the matter and recalls the matter at 1:40pm. Noting there being no obligation from opposing counsel to appear pursuant to the court's procedures, the court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF DR. BRIAN HANLEY's Motion To Compel Further Responses:

1. The court lacks a sufficient record to evaluate and rule on the motion. Plaintiff has failed to comply fully with California Rules of Court, Rule 3.1345 regarding the required contents of a separate statement supporting a discovery motion to compel. In particular, subdivision (c)(4) states that: "If necessary, the text of all the definitions, instructions, and other matters required to understand each discovery request and the responses to it." Plaintiff's separate statement does not provide the definitions of the capitalized terms that exist in each of the requests subject to this motion. Further, Plaintiff's Request and Defendant's response thereto are not part of the record. Therefore, the court cannot adequately understand and evaluate the requests. The motion is DENIED.

2. Based on the foregoing, the court makes no ruling on whether the requested documents are subject to discovery.

3. Neither party has made a request for sanctions and the court declines to issue any sanctions in regard to the instant motion.

Motion To Strike Portions Of Second Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Jun-17-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Craig Byrnes, Esq. (310.706.4177; cbyrnes_esq@yahoo.com) for Plaintiff.
Steven Piser, Esq. (510.835.5582; wiglaf@pacbell.net) for Defendant.

Not argued. The court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT CENTIVAX, INC.'S Motion To Strike Portions Of Second Amended Complaint.

Centivax's motion to strike is granted with 20 days leave to amend. Plaintiff has leave to amend to: (1) Delete the reference to CC 1636 in the UCL claim. CC 1636 is a canon of construction or aid to help the court interpret a contract. It is not a basis for liability under the UCL. (2) Delete the reference to CC 3294(c)(3). That provision defines "fraud" for the purpose of the punitive damages statute. Again, that provision is not a basis for liability under the UCL. (3) Remove the request for damages on the UCL claim. A party bringing a UCL claim can only recover restitution or an injunction. (See Korea Supply Co. v. Lockheed Martin Corp. (2003) 29 Cal.4th 1134, 1148; Business & Professions Code sec. 17203.) (4) Plaintiff may plead the proposed claim for receipt of stolen property. (5) Plaintiff also has leave to amend to clarify that he is seeking to plead a traditional alter ego theory of liability, i.e., that Glanville is liable for Centivax's debts/liabilities because there is a unity of interest. Plaintiff cannot plead the opposite reverse alter ego theory (Centivax is liable for Glanville's debts) under Postal Instant Press, Inc. v. Kaswa Corp. (2008) 162 Cal.App.4th 1510.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Off Calendar Jan-11-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES:
Atty. CRAIG T. BYRNES, Esq. of Law Office of Craig Ty. Byrnes (Tel: 310-706-4177) for plaintiff (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. STEVEN B. PISER, Esq. of Law Offices of Steven B. Piser (Tel: 510-835-5582) for defendant Centivax, Inc.;
Atty. JOHN L. FITZGERALD, Esq. of Law Offices of John L. Fitzgerald (Tel: 650-638-2386) for defendant Jacob Glanville.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT CENTIVAX, INC.'S DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Matter not contested/argued. Court and counsel discuss possible typographical error in the tentative ruling. The Court adopts its tentative ruling with the sole modification of clarifying that "this action does not involve an LLC," as follows:

Defendant Centivax, Inc.'s demurrer to the First Amended Complaint ("FAC") is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. Plaintiff apparently seeks to hold Centivax liable as the alter ego of Glanville. That theory of reverse piercing of the corporate veil appears to be unavailable in this case. First, this action does not involve an LLC. Second, the FAC indicates that Glanville is not 100% owner of Centivax. (See FAC, par. 5(b) [referring to Glanville's "all or a controlling interest"], Ex. 1 [reference to non-party Friede's ownership interest]; Postal Instant Press, Inc. v. Kaswa Corp. (2008) 162 Cal.App.4th 1510 [rejecting reverse piercing of the corporate veil theory]; Curci Investments, LLC v. Baldwin (2017) 14 Cal.App.5th 214 [allowing reverse piercing of LLC where individual and his wife were 100% owners of the LLC].) Plaintiff has leave to allege that Glanville is in fact 100% owner of Centivax or other facts showing direct or respondeat superior/vicarious liability against Centivax.

Counsel for Centivax, Inc. is required to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/RBU)
